hypotheses: A partner's attention toward a target person will increase the target's intimacy with the partner.
claim.hypotheses_location: Abstract (first paragraph) and Section 1.5 (titled 'Dissociating attention from benefit'), where the hypothesis is stated: 'The hypothesis of the present study was that a partner's attention toward a target person would increase the target's intimacy with the partner.'
claim.statement: A partner's attention toward a target person will increase the target's intimacy with the partner.
claim.statement_location: Abstract (first paragraph) and Section 1.5 (explicit statement of the hypothesis).

data.source: Primary data collected from human participants via scenario questionnaires (studies 1a and 1b) and laboratory behavioral experiments with questionnaire measures (studies 2a–2c).
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1: scenario experiments (1a and 1b). Study 2: laboratory experiments (2a, 2b, 2c).

sample_size.study_1a_reported: 312 recruited; 3 omitted for missing values (final N reported implicitly as 309).

data.access_details: Not stated (no direct data repository or public dataset link provided in the paper; supplementary data referred to via the article DOI).
data.notes: Measures: study 1 intimacy items rated on 5-point scales (three items per scenario; Cronbach's α .69–.80 per scenario; aggregated α=.76 for study 1a and α=.82 for study 1b). Study 2 intimacy measured with four items on 7-point scales (aggregated Cronbach's α = .72). Laboratory studies used a deceptive cover story (partner was a computer program) and manipulated attention via a visual signal with varying probabilities (.0, .20, .50, .80). Mediation analyses employed Sobel tests. No raw dataset or code is included in the paper; supplementary materials are referenced via the article DOI.

method.description: A series of experiments tested whether partner attentiveness increases perceived intimacy. Study 1 comprised two scenario (vignette) experiments manipulating attention (present/absent), benefit (present/absent), and time (long/short in 1a) and measuring intimacy (understanding, validation, caring) and social-exchange variables (expectation of partner help, willingness to help). Study 2 comprised three laboratory experiments in which participants performed a trivia task paired with an ostensible partner (actually a computer). Partner attention was indicated by a signal turning blue with specified probabilities; in all lab studies monitoring (attention) was dissociated from benefit (hints were disabled). Dependent measures included aggregated intimacy scales and interest in friendship. Statistical analyses included ANOVAs, t-tests, pairwise (Tukey) comparisons, correlations, and mediation (Sobel) tests.
steps: Develop support scenarios (advice, surprise, moral support) via pilot study and write vignette manipulations for attention and benefit.
Recruit undergraduate participants and administer scenario questionnaires (study 1a, study 1b) with counterbalanced scenario order (Latin square).
Aggregate intimacy items per scenario and compute reliability (Cronbach's alpha).

Analyze scenario data with factorial ANOVAs (attention × benefit × time in 1a; attention × benefit in 1b) and follow-up analyses per scenario; conduct mediation analyses (Sobel) with intimacy as mediator for social exchange outcomes.

Design laboratory trivia task with cover story assigning participant to player 2 and a simulated partner (computer) as player 1; program a visual signal to indicate partner monitoring with controlled probabilities (e.g., 0%, 20%, 50%, 80%).
Randomly assign participants to attention-probability conditions (study 2a: 0% vs 80%; study 2b: 80% with intentional vs 'no intention' control; study 2c: 20%/50%/80%).
Administer the trivia task, followed by post-task questionnaires measuring intimacy (4 items) and interest in friendship; debrief participants and provide payment.

Analyze lab data with t-tests (pairwise attention vs control), ANOVA for multiple attention levels, calculate effect sizes (Cohen's d, η2), correlations between actual/estimated attention frequency and intimacy, and Tukey HSD for post-hoc comparisons.
method.models: ANOVA (factorial ANOVAs reported for scenario and lab experiments), independent-samples t-tests for condition comparisons, Tukey HSD post-hoc tests, Pearson correlations, mediation analyses using Sobel tests. Effect-size metrics reported include η2 and Cohen's d.
method.outcome_variable: Primary: perceived intimacy (aggregated scale of items for understanding, validation, caring). Secondary: expectation of partner help, willingness to help (study 1); interest in friendship (study 2).
method.independent_variables: Primary: partner attention (manipulated: present vs absent; or frequency/probability 0%, 20%, 50%, 80%). Other manipulated variables: partner-provided benefit (present vs absent) and time spent (long vs short, in study 1a). Scenario order included as factor.
method.control_variables: Sex was measured and tested but produced no significant effects (not included in main reported analyses). Scenario order was included as a factor in ANOVAs. In study 2b a 'no intention' control (signal change determined by computer) controlled for intentionality of attention.
method.tools_software: Task implemented on laptop computers in the laboratory; specific statistical software or packages not stated.

results.summary: Across two scenario experiments and three laboratory experiments, partner attention robustly increased reported intimacy. In scenario studies, attention had a larger effect on intimacy than did the partner's benefit; intimacy mediated the effect of attention on expectation of partner help and willingness to help. In laboratory studies, participants who received frequent partner monitoring (attention) reported higher intimacy than controls; intimacy increased monotonically with attention frequency (20% < 50% < 80%). Effects were statistically significant with medium-to-large effect sizes. Interest in friendship was correlated with intimacy and was higher in attention conditions in some studies.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Expectation of partner help (attention effect) - Study 1a

numerical_results.direction: positive (higher attention associated with higher intimacy)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Correlation: actual attention frequency vs estimated frequency - Study 2c
Correlation: actual attention frequency vs intimacy - Study 2c

metadata.original_paper_title: It's the attention that counts: interpersonal attention fosters intimacy and social exchange
metadata.original_paper_data: Supplementary data referenced via article DOI (supplementary materials available online at the article DOI); no direct public dataset link provided in the article.
